It goes to the seller (secondary mkt). Pitfall 2 Stock = money market → FALSE. Stocks have no maturity; they're capital market. Pitfall 3 Derivatives = always risky → FALSE. They're tools for risk reduction OR speculation. Pitfall 4 FX risk only affects losses → FALSE. Currency moves can also generate gains. Pitfall 5 Secondary mkt = unimportant → FALSE. It enables primary mkt to function. The primary vs. secondary market distinction — especially who receives money in each case. This is a classic exam trap. The one-year maturity rule for money vs. capital markets, and be able to classify any instrument by type. The three functions of secondary markets: liquidity, price information, low transaction costs. The definition of a derivative: payoff linked to an underlying previously issued security. Know all four types. Foreign exchange risk definition and a simple numeric example of how currency movement affects dollar returns.

title: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates
subtitle: Financial Markets & Institutions
content: From loanable funds theory to the Fisher effect, yield curves, and time value of money — with worked examples and exam-ready exercises.

title: Why Interest Rates Matter
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Interest rates are among the most powerful forces in finance. They are the price of money — what you pay to borrow it and what you earn by lending it. Nominal interest rates are the rates actually observed in financial markets: the 5% on your savings account, the 7% on a mortgage, the 4% yield on a Treasury bond. These rates directly affect the price of virtually every financial security, and their movements ripple through every corner of the economy. When interest rates rise, existing bond prices fall (because new bonds offer higher yields, making old ones less valuable). When rates fall, borrowing becomes cheaper, stimulating business investment and consumer spending. Central banks like the Federal Reserve spend enormous energy trying to understand, forecast, and influence interest rates — which is why understanding where interest rates come from is foundational to all of finance. Background: Nominal vs. Real interest rates
          A "nominal" rate is the rate you actually see quoted. A "real" rate adjusts for inflation. If your savings account pays 5% but inflation is 3%, your real return is approximately 2% — you're only gaining 2% in actual purchasing power. This distinction is critical throughout Chapter 2 and reappears constantly in finance.

title: The Loanable Funds Theory
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The loanable funds theory is the foundational framework for understanding interest rate determination. It views equilibrium interest rates as the result of the supply of and demand for loanable funds in financial markets — just like the price of any good is determined by supply and demand. When supply of funds increases or demand decreases, interest rates fall. When demand increases or supply decreases, interest rates rise.

title: Suppliers of Loanable Funds
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The "supply of loanable funds" describes capital provided to financial markets by those with surplus money. Supply increases as interest rates rise — higher rates incentivize saving and lending. The major suppliers are:

title: What Shifts the Supply Curve
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
At any given interest rate, the supply of loanable funds shifts when these factors change: As overall wealth increases, people and institutions supply more funds. A growing economy generates more surplus capital looking for investment. Wealth ↑ → Supply ↑ If financial securities become riskier (more volatility, more defaults), suppliers pull back. Risk ↑ → Supply ↓ (risk-averse investors demand higher rates before they'll supply funds) If households anticipate upcoming expenses (holiday season, medical costs), they save more beforehand. Spending needs ↑ → Supply ↑ (temporarily, as they accumulate funds) When the Federal Reserve allows economic expansion (e.g., low rates, bond purchases), it injects money into the system. Expansionary policy → Supply ↑. Contractionary policy → Supply ↓ Shift Factor 5: Foreign economic conditions
          When domestic economic conditions improve, domestic suppliers have more to invest, increasing the local supply of funds. When foreign economies improve, foreign investors may redirect funds home rather than to U.S. markets — reducing the foreign supply of funds to U.S. markets. These are asymmetric effects worth remembering.

title: Demanders of Loanable Funds
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The "demand for loanable funds" describes the total net demand for borrowing by fund users. Demand generally increases as interest rates fall — cheaper borrowing costs make more projects viable. The major borrowers are:

title: What Shifts the Demand Curve
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Three key factors shift demand at any given rate: 1. Utility/return from borrowed funds. If the expected return from an investment (funded by borrowed money) rises, businesses are willing to borrow more even at the same rate. Expected project return ↑ → Demand ↑. 2. Non-price conditions on borrowing. Even if the interest rate stays the same, if banks require more collateral, impose restrictive covenants (limits on what borrowers can do with funds), or charge higher fees, effective borrowing becomes more expensive. More restrictive non-price conditions → Demand ↓. 3. Economic growth vs. stagnation. During economic expansion, businesses borrow more to invest in new capacity and inventory. During recessions, borrowing plummets because investment opportunities look poor. Growth → Demand ↑; Stagnation → Demand ↓. Common misconception: Government as supplier vs. demander
          Students often forget that governments appear on BOTH sides of the loanable funds market. The government can supply funds (when running a surplus or managing reserves) AND demand funds (when running a deficit, which is most of the time in the U.S.). In practice, the U.S. government is a massive net borrower, so it primarily shifts the demand curve rightward, putting upward pressure on interest rates — a phenomenon called "crowding out."

title: Determinants of Interest Rates for Individual Securities
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The loanable funds theory explains market-level interest rates. But why do different securities trade at different rates? A 10-year Treasury bond, a corporate junk bond, and a municipal bond all have different yields — even in the same market, at the same time. The answer lies in the components that make up any security's interest rate: Each component compensates the investor for a specific type of cost or risk. Let's unpack each one.

title: 1. Inflation Premium (IP) and the Fisher Effect
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Inflation is the continual increase in the price level of a basket of goods and services. It is measured in the U.S. using the Consumer Price Index (CPI) or Producer Price Index (PPI). The key insight: if you lend money at 5% for a year, but prices rise 4%, your purchasing power only grew by 1%. Investors demand compensation for expected inflation — the inflation premium (IP). The relationship between the real risk-free rate, expected inflation, and the nominal interest rate is captured by the Fisher effect, named after economist Irving Fisher: The real risk-free rate (RFR) is the interest rate that would exist on a risk-free security if no inflation were expected. It represents society's "time preference for money" — how much people prefer consumption today over future consumption. Higher preference for spending now → higher RFR. The RFR is theoretical; in practice, the U.S. Treasury bill rate (adjusted for inflation) is used as a proxy.

title: 2. Default Risk Premium (DRP)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Default risk is the risk that a security issuer will fail to make promised interest and principal payments. The higher the default risk, the higher the interest rate investors demand. The compensation for bearing this risk is the default risk premium (DRP): Background: Credit rating agencies
          Companies like Moody's, Standard & Poor's (S&P), and Fitch assign credit ratings to bond issuers. AAA/Aaa rated bonds are considered investment-grade with very low default risk; BB/Ba and below are "junk" or high-yield bonds with higher default risk — and correspondingly higher DRPs. The spread between AAA corporate bonds and Treasury yields fluctuates with economic conditions, widening sharply during crises.

title: 3. Liquidity Risk Premium (LRP)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Liquidity risk is the risk that a security cannot be sold quickly at a predictable price with low transaction costs. Highly liquid assets (like U.S. Treasury securities) trade constantly with tiny bid-ask spreads. Illiquid assets (like thinly traded municipal bonds) may require selling at a significant discount if you need cash quickly. Investors who accept illiquid securities demand a liquidity risk premium (LRP). Think of it this way: a house and a U.S. Treasury bond might offer the same annual "return," but you'd demand extra compensation for the house because selling it takes months. That extra compensation is the liquidity premium. Two faces of liquidity risk premium
          LRP can arise in two ways: (1) actual illiquidity — the security is hard to trade; and (2) maturity-related sensitivity — even liquid long-term bonds are riskier than short-term bonds because their prices are more sensitive to interest rate changes. Your notes specifically mention this second source. A 30-year bond's price drops far more than a 1-year bond when rates rise by the same amount — this duration risk is sometimes compensated with an LRP.

title: 4. Special Covenant/Feature Premium (SCP)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Certain contractual features written into a security's terms affect its interest rate. These provisions can either benefit or burden the investor: Key rule to remember
          Features that benefit the investor → lower interest rate (investor accepts less yield for the added value). Features that benefit the issuer → higher interest rate (investor demands more yield to compensate for the disadvantage). Callability is the classic "issuer benefit" that students often forget raises the yield.

title: Term Structure of Interest Rates and the Yield Curve
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The term structure of interest rates is a comparison of yields on securities that are identical in every way except their maturity. Plotted on a chart, this relationship is called the yield curve. The yield curve is one of the most important and closely watched tools in finance because its shape signals expectations about the economy and future interest rates. The change in required interest rates as maturity changes is called the maturity premium (MP). The MP can be positive, negative, or zero — which gives rise to three common yield curve shapes:

title: Three Theories Explaining Yield Curve Shape
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Long-term rates equal the average of expected future short-term rates. If the market expects short-term rates to rise, long-term rates will be higher today — creating an upward slope. If rates are expected to fall, the curve inverts. This theory implies the yield curve reflects pure expectations, nothing more. Extends expectations theory by adding a liquidity (risk) premium for longer maturities. Investors are risk-averse and dislike locking money up for long periods. So long-term rates = expected future short-term rates + a liquidity premium. This means yield curves will be upward biased even if rates are expected to stay flat. Different investors prefer different maturities (their "habitats") and won't easily move between maturity segments. Banks prefer short-term; pension funds prefer long-term. Supply and demand within each segment independently determines yields. The curve shape is driven by segment-specific supply/demand, not expectations. Why does an inverted yield curve matter so much?
          An inverted yield curve (short-term rates higher than long-term rates) is historically one of the most reliable predictors of a recession. It signals that the market expects the central bank to cut rates in the future (because the economy will weaken), making long-term rates lower than short-term rates today. Every U.S. recession since 1955 was preceded by an inverted yield curve. Expectations Theory Calculation: Example
          Suppose the 1-year rate today is 3%, the expected 1-year rate next year is 4%, and the expected 1-year rate in two years is 5%. Under the Unbiased Expectations Theory, the 3-year rate today should be approximately (3% + 4% + 5%) / 3 = 4%. The yield curve is upward-sloping because rates are expected to rise.

title: Time Value of Money (TVM)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The time value of money is the foundational concept underlying all of finance: a dollar received today is worth more than a dollar received in the future. Why? Because a dollar today can be invested to earn a return, making it worth more than a dollar received later. This creates a framework for comparing cash flows that occur at different points in time. The 3 Rules of Time Travel (from your TVM supplement)
          Rule 1: You can only compare/combine cash flows at the same point in time. Rule 2: To move a cash flow forward, you must compound it (multiply by (1+r)). Rule 3: To move a cash flow backward, you must discount it (divide by (1+r)). Everything in TVM follows from these three rules.

title: Lump Sum Formulas
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: FV Example: Invest $1,000 today at 10% for 2 years: PV Example: Need $10,000 in 1 year. Can earn 7%. How much to invest today?

title: Annuity Formulas
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: An annuity is a series of equal cash flows received at fixed intervals over a finite number of periods. Your mortgage payment, car payment, or annual bond coupon are all annuities. A bond pays $50 per year for 3 years, then $1,000 at maturity. Discount rate = 6%. Find PV of the coupon payments.

title: Perpetuity Formulas
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A perpetuity is an annuity that pays forever. It sounds complex, but the formula is beautifully simple:

title: Solving for Rate (r) and Time (n)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Critical: Simple vs. Compound Interest
          Simple interest earns interest only on the principal. Compound interest earns interest on principal PLUS previously earned interest. At $100 × 5% for 2 years: Simple FV = $110. Compound FV = $110.25. The difference grows enormously over long periods. At 200 years: Simple = $1,200. Compound = $4,471,898. Every finance problem assumes compound interest unless explicitly told otherwise. Calculator sign convention (critical for avoiding errors)
          On the BA-II Plus, cash outflows are negative, inflows are positive. If you invest $1,000 today (outflow), enter PV = −1,000. The calculator will return FV as a positive number (you receive it). Always clear TVM registers (2ND → FV) between problems. Forgetting the sign convention is the #1 cause of calculator errors on exams. Imagine a snowball rolling downhill. The bigger it gets, the more snow it picks up — so it keeps accelerating. Compound interest works the same way. Each year, you earn interest not just on your original money, but also on all the interest you've already earned. So the pile grows, which means next year's interest is even bigger, which makes the pile even bigger, and so on forever. Put $100 at 10%. After year 1: $110. After year 2: not $120 (simple) but $121 — because you earned 10% on $110, not just $100. That extra $1 seems trivial. But over 30 years, your $100 becomes $1,745 with compounding, versus $400 with simple interest. The snowball effect is enormous. A snowball eventually gets too big to roll and stops. Money, in theory, can compound forever without stopping (though inflation, taxes, and market returns vary in practice). Also, compounding requires reinvesting returns — if you spend your interest each year, it's essentially simple interest. Compound interest applies the interest rate to the growing accumulated balance, not just the original principal. The future value formula is FV = PV × (1+r)ⁿ. The exponent n is what creates the exponential (not linear) growth. Simple interest grows as FV = PV × (1 + r×n), which is linear. The mathematical difference between linear and exponential growth is what makes compounding so powerful over long horizons. Imagine you lend your friend $100 so they can buy you pizza when they repay you next year. But you know pizza will cost 10% more next year due to inflation. If your friend just gives back $100, you can buy less pizza than you could today. So you'd tell your friend: "Pay me $110 — $100 to replace what I lent you, and $10 to cover the fact that pizza costs more now." That extra $10 is the inflation premium. Lenders in financial markets do exactly this: they add expected inflation to their required return so they don't lose purchasing power. If I want a 2% real return and expect 3% inflation, I'll demand 5% nominal. The real world is more complex because the actual inflation that occurs may differ from expected inflation. Lenders are compensated for expected inflation at the time of the loan; if actual inflation turns out higher, they lose purchasing power anyway. Also, the Fisher effect is empirically approximate — the precise relationship involves a multiplicative cross-product term that matters at high inflation rates. The Fisher effect states: (1+i) = (1+RFR) × (1+E(IP)), where i is the nominal rate, RFR is the real risk-free rate, and E(IP) is expected inflation. The approximation i ≈ RFR + E(IP) ignores the cross-product term RFR×E(IP), which is small at low rates but significant at high rates. The Fisher effect implies that nominal rates should rise one-for-one with changes in expected inflation, holding the real rate constant — an empirically tested prediction with strong (but imperfect) support in the data. Imagine there's a rumor that next summer will be extremely hot. Water vendors right now charge $1 per bottle (that's the current "short-term price"). But vendors willing to lock in a price for next summer are charging $3 per bottle because of the expected shortage. That "forward price" is higher because people expect conditions to change. The yield curve works similarly. If people expect interest rates to rise in the future (because the economy will be booming), they demand higher yields today on long-term loans. Why? Because if rates rise in two years, a long-term bond paying today's rate will look stingy. The curve slopes upward. If people expect a recession (rates to fall), the curve may invert — short-term rates are high now but expected to fall. The yield curve reflects multiple forces simultaneously, not just expectations: risk premiums for holding long-term bonds, segmented market supply/demand, Federal Reserve policy interventions, and global capital flows. No single theory fully explains all yield curve shapes at all times — that's why three competing theories exist. The term structure of interest rates plots yields against maturities for securities with identical characteristics except maturity. The maturity premium (MP) can be positive, negative, or zero. Three theories explain its shape: (1) Unbiased Expectations — long rates equal the geometric average of expected future short rates; (2) Liquidity Premium — long rates include a risk premium above expectations; (3) Market Segmentation — supply and demand in each maturity "habitat" independently determines yields, with no arbitrage across segments. Suppose someone offers you a choice: $1,000 right now, or a guaranteed $1,000 exactly one year from now. Any rational person takes the money now — because you can invest it and have more than $1,000 in a year. The future $1,000 is worth less to you today because you're giving up the ability to earn a return on it. To compare them fairly, we need to ask: "How much would I need to invest today to have $1,000 in one year?" If you can earn 10%, the answer is $1,000 ÷ 1.10 = $909.09. So the present value of $1,000 received in one year at 10% is $909.09. We "discounted" the future value back to today's terms. This assumes certainty — guaranteed future payments. In reality, future cash flows are usually uncertain. The discount rate used in practice incorporates not just the risk-free rate but also risk premiums to account for that uncertainty. Riskier cash flows get discounted at higher rates, making their present value lower — reflecting that investors demand compensation for uncertainty. Present value (PV) is the current value of a future cash flow, discounted at an appropriate rate. For a lump sum: PV = FV/(1+r)ⁿ. For a stream of cash flows: PV = Σ Cₙ/(1+r)ⁿ. The discount rate r represents the opportunity cost of capital — the return available on the best alternative investment of comparable risk. Higher r → lower PV. Longer horizon → lower PV. This framework underlies all of security valuation, capital budgeting, and financial decision-making. Equilibrium interest rates are determined by the supply of and demand for loanable funds in financial markets — exactly like price determination in a product market. When supply of funds exceeds demand, rates fall; when demand exceeds supply, rates rise. The theory categorizes all market participants (consumers, businesses, governments, foreign participants) as either net suppliers or demanders of funds. ij* = f(IP, RFR, DRPj, LRPj, SCPj, MPj) (1) IP — Inflation Premium: compensates for expected loss in purchasing power
            (2) RFR — Real Risk-Free Rate: base return for time preference with no risk/inflation
            (3) DRP — Default Risk Premium: compensates for risk of issuer failing to pay
            (4) LRP — Liquidity Risk Premium: compensates for difficulty selling at fair price quickly
            (5) SCP — Special Covenant Premium: adjusts for features like callability (↑ rate) or tax-exemption (↓ rate)
            (6) MP — Maturity Premium: accounts for sensitivity to rate changes with longer term Compound FV = PV × (1+r)ⁿ = 1,000 × (1.08)³ = 1,000 × 1.2597 = $1,259.71 Simple FV = PV × (1 + r×n) = 1,000 × (1 + 0.08×3) = 1,000 × 1.24 = $1,240.00 Difference: $19.71. This gap grows significantly over longer horizons — compound interest's "snowball effect" becomes increasingly powerful over time. At 6%: 72 / 6 = 12 years
            At 12%: 72 / 12 = 6 years Doubling the interest rate cuts the doubling time in half. Check: $1,000 at 6% for 12 years = 1,000 × (1.06)¹² = $2,012 ✓ (approximately doubles). The Rule of 72 is a quick mental math shortcut — handy on exams when you need a rough estimate without a calculator. PV = C / r = 500 / 0.05 = $10,000 This is elegantly simple: a stream of $500 per year forever, discounted at 5%, is worth $10,000 today. Makes intuitive sense — $10,000 invested at 5% generates $500/year in interest, so $10,000 is the fair price for that cash flow stream. Approximate: i ≈ RFR + E(IP) = 1.5% + 2.8% = 4.3% Exact (Fisher): (1+i) = (1.015)(1.028) = 1.04342, so i = 4.342% The cross-product (0.015 × 0.028 = 0.000042 = 0.042%) is very small at these rate levels, so the approximation works well. At 20% inflation and 5% real rate, the difference would be 1% — much more significant. Formula: PV = FV / (1+r)ⁿ = 150,000 / (1.08)¹⁷ (1.08)¹⁷ = 3.7000 (approx) PV = 150,000 / 3.7000 = $40,540.34 Calculator: N=17, I/Y=8, FV=150,000, PMT=0 → CPT PV = -40,540.34 Investing ~$40,500 today at 8% grows to $150,000 in 17 years. This is the power of early saving and compound growth. Formula: PV = C × [1 − 1/(1+r)ⁿ] / r = 350 × [1 − 1/(1.005)⁴⁸] / 0.005 (1.005)⁴⁸ = 1.2705; 1/1.2705 = 0.7871; 1 − 0.7871 = 0.2129 = 350 × 0.2129 / 0.005 = 350 × 42.58 = $14,904.22 Calculator: N=48, I/Y=0.5, PMT=350, FV=0 → CPT PV = -14,904.22 Note: When payments are monthly, the period interest rate must be monthly (6%/12 = 0.5%) and N is the number of months, not years. DRP = Corporate yield − Treasury yield = 4.5% − 3.8% = 0.70% (70 basis points) The DRP compensates investors for the (small, since AAA) risk that the corporation might default, compared to the effectively zero-default risk of the U.S. Treasury. Callability: The yield would be HIGHER. A callable bond gives the issuer the right to repay early — typically when rates fall. This hurts the investor (who loses the above-market coupon just when they'd most want it). Investors demand a higher yield (call premium) to compensate for this disadvantage. Callable bonds always have higher yields than equivalent non-callable bonds. Exact calculation: (1 + 3yr rate)³ = (1.04)(1.05)(1.06) = 1.15668 3yr rate = (1.15668)^(1/3) − 1 = 1.04993 − 1 = 4.993% ≈ 5% Approximation: (4% + 5% + 6%) / 3 = 5% The 3-year yield today (≈5%) is higher than the current 1-year rate (4%) because the market expects rates to rise. This creates an upward-sloping yield curve at these maturities. (a) Supply of funds: Higher interest rates incentivize more saving (higher return for deferring consumption). Supply curve shifts rightward — more funds available at each rate level. Foreign investors may also move funds into U.S. markets for higher yields, increasing supply further. (b) Demand for funds: Higher rates make borrowing more expensive. Businesses postpone investments that no longer meet return thresholds (projects must earn more than the higher borrowing cost). Household demand for mortgages and consumer credit falls. Demand curve shifts leftward — fewer funds demanded at each rate level. (c) Fed's strategy: By tightening monetary policy, the Fed is deliberately reducing the supply of reserves and raising rates, which: (1) reduces spending/investment (cools the economy); (2) reduces the demand for funds; and (3) works through the Fisher effect — by bringing actual inflation down, it reduces the inflation premium component of all interest rates, eventually allowing nominal rates to fall. The mechanism is intentional monetary contraction to reduce aggregate demand and cool price pressures. Unbiased Expectations Theory: Long-term rates = average of expected future short-term rates. Predicts flat curves when rates are expected to stay flat, inverted when expected to fall. Assumes risk-neutral investors who are indifferent between short and long maturities. Liquidity Premium Theory: Extends expectations by adding a positive risk premium for longer maturities. Even if rates are expected to stay flat, the curve slopes upward because investors demand extra return for holding longer-term, less liquid securities. Explains why curves are usually upward-sloping. Market Segmentation Theory: Investors and institutions prefer specific maturities based on their liability structures. Banks prefer short-term (matching short-term deposits); pension funds prefer long-term (matching long-term obligations). Supply and demand within each segment determines yields independently. The shape reflects segment-specific imbalances, not expectations. Best explanation for persistent upward slope: Liquidity Premium Theory. It preserves the expectations logic while adding a realistic behavioral element: investors are risk-averse and demand compensation for holding longer, more volatile securities. This naturally biases the curve upward even when rates are expected to be flat — which matches empirical data far better than the pure expectations theory. Why munis yield less: Municipal bond interest is exempt from federal income tax (and often state/local taxes in the issuing state). This is a "special feature" (SCP) that benefits the investor. Under the SCP framework, features that benefit investors → lower interest rate. Investors accept a lower nominal yield on munis because their after-tax return can equal or exceed a higher-yielding taxable bond's after-tax return. Calculation illustration: An investor in the 35% tax bracket compares a 4% muni to a 6% corporate bond. After-tax corporate return = 6% × (1−0.35) = 3.9%. After-tax muni return = 4% (no tax). The muni wins despite the lower stated yield. The tax-equivalent yield formula: Tax-equiv yield = Muni yield / (1 − tax rate) = 4% / 0.65 = 6.15%. So the muni is equivalent to a 6.15% taxable bond. Best investor for munis: High-income investors in high tax brackets — they gain the most from the tax exemption. Low-income investors (low tax bracket) benefit less from tax exemption and may find taxable bonds better. Tax-exempt institutional investors (pension funds, IRAs) gain nothing from the exemption and should avoid munis entirely. (a) PV = C/(r−g) = 100/(0.08−0.03) = 100/0.05 = $2,000 (b) If g rises to 5%: PV = 100/(0.08−0.05) = 100/0.03 = $3,333Higher growth rate → smaller denominator → much higher PV. A 2% increase in growth rate increased value by 67%! (c) If r falls to 5% (g stays at 3%): PV = 100/(0.05−0.03) = 100/0.02 = $5,000The denominator (r−g) narrows from 0.05 to 0.02, more than doubling the value. This shows why falling interest rates cause massive increases in asset prices — they reduce the discount rate that shrinks all future cash flows. Warning: If r ≤ g, the formula breaks down (you get zero or negative denominator). This is theoretically impossible for a real asset — in practice, growth rates must be below the discount rate in the long run. Your friend has a point, but it's more nuanced: Why they may be right (current risk): When rates are high, long-term bond prices are low (inverse relationship). If you buy now and rates rise further, bond prices fall more — and long-term bonds fall far more than short-term bonds for the same rate increase (this is the maturity premium / duration effect). If you need to sell before maturity, you could face capital losses. Why they may be wrong (opportunity): High rates today mean high yields — you're locking in a high return for many years. If rates fall (which often happens after rate hikes), your bond's price rises and you can sell at a capital gain. Historically, after the Fed tightens aggressively, rates eventually fall, creating substantial capital gains on long-term bonds. Additional risks to consider: DRP — is the bond's issuer creditworthy? During rate-hike cycles, economic stress can increase default risk on corporate bonds. LRP — can you sell the bond quickly if needed? Long-term corporate bonds can be illiquid in stressed markets. Reinvestment risk — if rates fall, future coupon reinvestment earns less. Conclusion: Whether it's a "bad time" depends on your investment horizon and whether you hold to maturity. If held to maturity, interest rate risk is irrelevant (you get par value + coupons regardless). If you might need to sell early, high-rate environments are indeed risky for long-term bonds. IP Expected inflation — measured by CPI/PPI changes RFR Time preference for money with no risk. Proxy: inflation-adjusted T-bill rate DRP Yield − equivalent Treasury yield. Higher for lower-rated issuers LRP Premium for illiquid securities or maturity-related price sensitivity SCP Tax-exempt → rate ↓. Callable → rate ↑. Convertible → rate ↓ MP Can be +, −, or 0. Rates expected to rise. Most common shape. Inverted ↓ Short-term > long-term. Rates expected to fall. Predicts recession. Flat → All maturities similar yield. Transition between normal and inverted. Expectations Long rate = avg of expected future short rates. Risk neutral. Liquidity Prem Adds risk premium to expectations. Biases curve upward. Market Seg Each maturity independently priced by its supply/demand segment. Know all 6 components of ij* — able to name, define, and say what causes each to increase or decrease. Apply the Fisher effect (both approximate and exact) and understand what each variable means. Calculate FV and PV of lump sums and annuities by formula AND by calculator (with correct sign convention). Explain the three yield curve theories — compare and contrast, and know which explains the persistent upward slope. Identify how supply/demand shifts affect interest rates in the loanable funds framework (who supplies, who demands, what shifts each curve).

title: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation
subtitle: Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Bond pricing, stock valuation, YTM, dividend discount models, duration, and convexity — with worked examples and numerical exercises.

title: The Big Picture: Valuing Financial Securities
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Chapter 3 is where the theoretical foundation of Chapters 1 and 2 becomes practical. You now know that interest rates exist and how they're determined — the question becomes: given an interest rate, what is a financial security actually worth? The answer always comes back to the same principle: the fair value of any financial security is the present value of all the cash flows it is expected to generate, discounted at the appropriate required rate of return. Everything in this chapter is an application of that one idea.

title: Three Interest Rate Measures: Required, Expected, Realized
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Before diving into valuation, your course distinguishes three different "interest rates" on a security. Students often confuse these, but they are conceptually distinct and describe different moments in time: The rate you should earn given the security's risk. Used to calculate fair present value. Reflects default risk, liquidity risk, maturity, and all other risk factors from Chapter 2. If market price = fair value, then r = E(r). The rate you expect to earn if you buy the security at its current market price, receive all projected cash flows, and sell at your horizon. This is the internal rate of return implied by current market price. Also called yield to maturity (YTM) for bonds. The rate you actually earned on an investment that has already happened. May differ from required return because of unexpected events — price changes, defaults, early calls. If r̄ > r, you earned more than required; if r̄ < r, you underperformed.

title: Market Efficiency: Why Required ≈ Expected
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
In an efficient market, prices adjust quickly to new information so that current market price ≈ fair present value. This means E(r) ≈ r most of the time — you can't consistently find securities where the expected return exceeds the required return (that would be a "free lunch"). When unexpected events occur (surprise earnings, Fed rate changes, credit downgrades), prices temporarily diverge from fair value, then quickly converge again as traders act on the mispricing. Background: Why this distinction matters
      In bond markets, E(r) = YTM is computed from market price. In stock markets, E(r) is harder to compute because dividends are uncertain. When a bond's YTM (E(r)) is higher than its required rate (r), the bond is underpriced — a buying opportunity. When YTM < r, the bond is overpriced. Market efficiency says such gaps close quickly as investors exploit them.

title: Bond Valuation
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A bond is a debt instrument where the issuer (borrower) promises to pay the holder (lender): (1) periodic coupon payments (interest) over the life of the bond, and (2) the face value (par value) — typically $1,000 — at maturity. The fair price of a bond equals the present value of all these promised cash flows, discounted at the required rate of return.

title: Coupon Bonds
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Semiannual compounding (most U.S. bonds)
      Most U.S. bonds pay coupons twice per year. For semiannual bonds: use r_b/2 as the period rate, and 2N as the number of periods. Coupon payment = (Coupon Rate × Face Value) / 2. Always check whether a problem specifies annual or semiannual compounding — it matters. A bond has a 8% coupon rate, $1,000 face value, 3 years to maturity, annual payments, and required return = 10%.

title: Zero-Coupon Bonds
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A zero-coupon bond (also called a pure discount bond) makes no periodic coupon payments — INT = 0. You buy it at a discount and receive only the face value at maturity. The entire return comes from the price appreciation. U.S. Treasury Bills are the best-known example. A zero-coupon bond with $100,000 face value is priced at $96,618.36 with 1-year maturity. What is the YTM?

title: Premium, Discount, and Par Bonds
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The relationship between a bond's coupon rate and required rate of return determines whether it trades at a premium, discount, or par. This is one of the most tested relationships in the chapter: Bond pays less than market demands → price falls below par. YTM > coupon rate. Bond pays exactly what market requires → trades at face value. YTM = coupon rate. Bond pays more than market requires → price rises above par. YTM < coupon rate.

title: Yield to Maturity (YTM)
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The Yield to Maturity (YTM) is the single discount rate that equates the present value of all promised cash flows to the current market price. It is the bond's expected rate of return if: (1) held to maturity, (2) all coupons are reinvested at the YTM rate. YTM is solved iteratively (trial and error) or by financial calculator: YTM assumption: reinvestment at YTM rate
      YTM assumes all coupon payments are reinvested at the YTM rate until maturity. If you actually reinvest at a different rate, your realized return will differ from YTM. This "reinvestment risk" is a key limitation of YTM as a measure of actual bond return.

title: Equity (Stock) Valuation
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Valuing stocks is conceptually similar to valuing bonds — find the present value of all future cash flows. The difference: bond cash flows (coupons + face value) are contractually fixed. Stock cash flows (dividends) are uncertain, can grow, and theoretically continue forever. The framework for this is the Dividend Discount Model (DDM). The price of any stock equals the present value of all future dividends it will pay, discounted at the equity cost of capital (r_E). P₀ = Σ Div_t / (1+r_E)^t for t = 1 to ∞ Since we can't enumerate infinite dividends, we use simplifying assumptions about dividend growth patterns:

title: Case 1: Zero Dividend Growth (Perpetuity)
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Dividends remain constant forever — like a preferred stock with a fixed dividend:

title: Case 2: Constant Growth (Gordon Growth Model)
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Dividends grow at a constant rate g forever — appropriate for mature, stable companies: A stock just paid a dividend of $2.00 (Div₀). Dividends grow at 5% forever. Required return = 10%. Critical: g must be less than r_E
      If g ≥ r_E, the Gordon Growth Model gives a nonsensical result (zero or negative denominator → infinite or negative price). This is impossible in equilibrium. The long-run growth rate of any company must be less than the economy's growth rate, which in turn must be less than the discount rate. When using this model, always verify g < r_E.

title: Case 3: Supernormal (Nonconstant) Dividend Growth
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Many companies — especially young, fast-growing firms — go through a period of high (above-normal) dividend growth before settling into steady-state growth. The three-step process handles this: Step 1: Calculate each dividend during the high-growth period and find its PV individually: PV(Divₜ) = Divₜ / (1+r_E)^t. Sum these PVs. Step 2: Find the stock price at the END of the high-growth period (Pₙ) using the Gordon Growth Model with the stable long-run growth rate: Pₙ = Divₙ₊₁ / (r_E − g_stable). Then discount Pₙ back to today: PV(Pₙ) = Pₙ / (1+r_E)ⁿ. Step 3: P₀ = Sum of PVs from Step 1 + PV(Pₙ) from Step 2. A stock pays no dividend for 2 years, then Div₃ = $1.00 growing at 25% for 2 years (Div₄ = $1.25), then constant 6% forever. r_E = 12%. Total Return Components
      For any stock: r_E = Dividend Yield + Capital Gain Rate, where Dividend Yield = Div₁/P₀ and Capital Gain Rate = (P₁−P₀)/P₀. In the constant-growth model, capital gain rate = g, which is why g also represents price appreciation. Investors receive value both from dividends paid and from price appreciation over time.

title: Impact of Interest Rate Changes on Bond Prices
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The relationship between interest rates and bond prices is one of the most fundamental in fixed income: when interest rates rise, bond prices fall; when rates fall, prices rise. This inverse relationship has three important nuances:

title: 1. The Relationship Is Non-Linear (Convex)
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: As interest rates increase, bond prices decrease — but at a decreasing rate. This means a 1% rate increase reduces prices by less than a 1% rate decrease raises them. The price-rate curve is bowed (convex), not a straight line. This non-linearity is measured by convexity (covered below).

title: 2. Maturity Effect: Longer Bonds Are More Sensitive
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The further a bond is from maturity, the more its price changes for a given interest rate move. A 30-year bond's price falls far more when rates rise 1% than a 2-year bond's price. However, this relationship is also non-linear — increasing maturity increases price sensitivity, but at a decreasing rate. Going from 1 year to 2 years adds more sensitivity than going from 29 to 30 years.

title: 3. Coupon Rate Effect: Lower-Coupon Bonds Are More Sensitive
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The higher the coupon rate, the less price-sensitive the bond. Why? Higher coupons return more cash to investors early (sooner in the bond's life). Since early cash flows are less affected by distant rate changes, high-coupon bonds are "weighted" toward the near future — making them less sensitive. Zero-coupon bonds have the highest price sensitivity of any bond with a given maturity.

title: Duration: The Unified Measure of Price Sensitivity
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Duration is the weighted-average time to maturity of a bond, using the relative present values of cash flows as weights. It is simultaneously: (1) a measure of the average life of a bond; and (2) a direct measure of the bond's price sensitivity to interest rate changes. 8% annual coupon bond, $1,000 face value, 3 years to maturity, required return = 10%.

title: Key Duration Features
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Higher coupon → shorter duration. Higher coupons return cash sooner, shifting the weight of cash flows toward the near future. Zero-coupon bond: duration = maturity (the longest possible for any given maturity). Higher required return → shorter duration. Higher discount rates reduce the relative weight of distant cash flows (the face value). This means higher-rate environments produce lower duration bonds. Longer maturity → longer duration, but at a decreasing rate. As maturity increases, duration grows but asymptotically approaches a ceiling. For a perpetuity: D = (1+r)/r. For a zero-coupon bond: Duration = Maturity. There's only one cash flow (face value at maturity), so the weighted average time is simply the maturity. Zero-coupon bonds have the maximum duration for their maturity.

title: Convexity: When Duration Isn't Enough
subtitle: Chapter 3: Interest Rates & Security Valuation · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Duration provides a linear approximation of how bond prices change with interest rates. But the actual price-rate relationship is curved (non-linear). Convexity is the degree of curvature of the price-interest rate curve around some interest rate level. It measures how much the duration approximation errors as rates change. Two critical insights about convexity: Duration overestimates price losses when rates rise and underestimates price gains when rates fall. The actual price is always better than the duration-predicted price — which means convexity is always good for the holder. Greater convexity means more protection against rate increases (price falls less than duration predicts) and greater gains when rates fall (price rises more). Investors should prefer higher-convexity bonds, all else equal. The larger the interest rate change, the more convexity causes duration's linear approximation to misestimate the actual price change. For small moves (a few basis points), duration is accurate. For large moves (100+ bps), use both duration and convexity. Every bond's price-rate relationship is convex because bond prices are the sum of discounted cash flows — a non-linear function of the discount rate. Convexity is not optional; it's a mathematical property of all bonds. Duration is most accurate for "small" changes — defined as one basis point = 0.01% (one-hundredth of 1%). For changes of a few basis points, duration alone is fine. For changes of 1% or more, convexity correction becomes important. Imagine you lent your friend $1,000 for a year, and they promised to pay you back $1,050 (5% interest). Now imagine the next day, everyone else starts lending money at 10% interest. Your friend's promise to pay you $1,050 is worth less than a fresh 10% loan — if someone else wanted to take over your loan, they'd only pay you something less than $1,000 because a new loan would earn them more. Your bond lost value even though nothing changed about the promise. That's the entire inverse relationship in a nutshell. When market rates rise, existing bonds (with their fixed coupons) become less attractive compared to new bonds — so their price falls. When rates fall, existing bonds look great — so buyers bid up their price. The analogy is actually quite accurate! The key nuance: the longer your friend's loan runs (longer maturity), the bigger the price impact of the rate change. A 1-day loan is barely affected by rate changes; a 30-year loan is hugely affected. Also, if your friend promises very frequent payments (high coupon), you get money back quickly, so you're less exposed to future rate changes. Bond price is the present value of all promised cash flows discounted at the required rate of return: P = Σ CF_t/(1+r)^t. When r increases, all discount factors (1+r)^t increase, reducing each PV term and thus total price. The relationship is not linear but convex — price falls at a decreasing rate as rates rise. This is because as rates rise, the present value of distant cash flows shrinks relatively faster than near-term flows, changing the weighting. Think of a bond's cash flows as weights on a seesaw. Each coupon is a small weight placed at its respective year on the seesaw. The big face-value payment is a heavy weight at maturity. Duration is the balance point — where would you have to put your finger to balance the entire seesaw? A zero-coupon bond has one heavy weight right at maturity — the balance point is at maturity. That's why duration = maturity for zeros. A high-coupon bond has lots of small weights spread out near the front — so the balance point is much closer to today. That's why high coupons mean shorter duration. The "weights" aren't the raw cash flows but their present values — larger discount rates shrink the far-away weights. This is why higher interest rates also shorten duration (far-away cash flows get discounted more, reducing their relative weight). The seesaw doesn't use raw cash flows; it uses PV-adjusted cash flows. Duration = weighted average time to maturity, where the weights are each cash flow's PV share of the total bond price. Formula: D = [Σ t × PV(CF_t)] / P. Duration doubles as a direct elasticity measure: ΔP/P ≈ −MD × Δr, where Modified Duration MD = D/(1+r). A bond with MD = 5 loses approximately 5% of its value for each 1% rise in rates. This makes duration the single most important risk measure for fixed-income securities. Imagine a golden goose that lays $100 worth of eggs per year, and you expect this to grow 3% every year forever. If you need a 10% return on your investment, how much would you pay for the goose? The answer is $100/(10%−3%) = $100/7% ≈ $1,429. Now change the growth rate to 8%. You'd pay $100/(10%−8%) = $100/2% = $5,000. A tiny change in expected growth (3% → 8%) multiplied the goose's value by 3.5x! This is why stock valuations are so sensitive to growth assumptions — small changes in g create massive changes in value because g appears in the denominator. The model assumes growth is constant forever, which is unrealistic. Real companies go through phases: high growth, then maturity, sometimes decline. The model also doesn't capture companies that pay no dividends. Growth rates must always stay below the discount rate, which doesn't apply to individual company growth rates in the short term. The Constant Growth DDM (Gordon Growth Model): P₀ = Div₁/(r_E − g), where Div₁ = next period's dividend, r_E = equity required return, g = perpetual growth rate. Rearranging: r_E = Div₁/P₀ + g = dividend yield + capital gain rate. This model is the cornerstone of equity valuation. For nonconstant growth, use the 3-step process: find PVs of dividends in the high-growth phase, then use the GGM to find terminal value at the end of that phase, and discount everything back. Imagine you're measuring how steep a hill is at one particular point. Duration is like saying "the hill slopes at 5 degrees right here." If you walk 1 meter in that direction, duration predicts you'll descend a certain amount. But the hill is curved — it gets gentler as you walk. So if you go 10 meters, duration's prediction becomes increasingly wrong. For bond prices: duration gives you the slope at one rate level. But as rates change, the slope itself changes. The actual bond price curve is always above the duration tangent line — meaning the bond always does better than duration predicts. This "always better than expected" property is what makes convexity valuable. Unlike a physical hill where you might walk any distance, convexity is most relevant for large interest rate movements (1% or more). For small moves (a few basis points), duration alone is fine. Also, some exotic instruments (like callable bonds) can have negative convexity — their price-rate curve bows in the opposite direction — but all standard coupon and zero-coupon bonds have positive convexity. Convexity is the second derivative of the price-rate relationship (duration is the first derivative). The full approximation: ΔP/P ≈ −MD×Δr + ½×Convexity×(Δr)². The second term is always positive (½×Convexity×(Δr)² > 0), meaning the actual price is always above the duration-only prediction. Properties: (1) Convexity is desirable; (2) Greater convexity → more accurate duration; (3) All standard bonds are convex; (4) Zero-coupon bonds have more convexity than coupon bonds of the same maturity. (1) Required Rate of Return (r) — Ex ante. The rate an investor SHOULD earn given a security's risk. Used to compute fair present value. From Chapter 2's interest rate determinants.
        (2) Expected Rate of Return E(r) — Ex ante. The rate an investor EXPECTS to earn by buying at current market price and holding. For bonds, this is the YTM. If markets are efficient, E(r) ≈ r.
        (3) Realized Rate of Return (r̄) — Ex post. The rate actually EARNED after the investment period. May differ from required return due to unexpected events. Lower coupon → less cash returned early → cash flows are weighted further into the future → longer weighted-average life (duration). The extreme case is a zero-coupon bond: all weight is at year 5, giving duration = 5. (c) Bond A is more price-sensitive. Higher duration → higher modified duration → larger percentage price change for any given rate change. Bond A's price swings more for the same rate movement. This is why low-coupon bonds are "riskier" in terms of interest rate risk. (a) 2-year high-coupon bond: Short maturity + high coupon → low duration (perhaps 1.5–1.8 years). Modified duration ≈ 1.5. Price falls approximately 1.5%. Relatively modest impact because cash flows are near-term and high in size. (b) 20-year zero-coupon bond: Duration = maturity = 20 years. Modified duration ≈ 20/1.0x ≈ 19. Price falls approximately 19%. Catastrophic impact — all cash flow is 20 years away and is now discounted much more heavily. This is the most affected of the three by far. (c) High-growth stock: In the DDM, P₀ = Div₁/(r_E − g). If r_E rises by 1% and g is fixed, the denominator (r_E − g) grows. For a stock with g = 9% and r_E = 10%, the price was 20× Div₁. After rate rise: r_E = 11%, price = Div₁/(0.11−0.09) = 50× Div₁. Wait — the denominator SHRANK from 1% to 2%! Price doubles. But this is a rate decrease example. A rate increase (r_E goes from 10% to 11%) with g = 9%: 10%−9%=1% denominator → 11%−9%=2% denominator. Price falls by 50%! High-growth stocks are enormously sensitive to rate changes because the (r_E − g) spread is tiny. Most affected overall: The 20-year zero-coupon bond (~19% loss) or high-growth stock (potentially 30–50% loss depending on g), with the short-term high-coupon bond barely moving (~1.5% loss). The asymmetry argument: Duration predicts equal price changes for equal rate changes in either direction. But convexity means the actual price curve bends away from this linear prediction — always favorably for the bondholder. Concretely: If rates rise by 1%, a high-convexity bond loses LESS than duration predicts. If rates fall by 1%, a high-convexity bond GAINS MORE than duration predicts. This asymmetry — "less pain when rates rise, more gain when rates fall" — is pure value for the bondholder. Why pay more? Imagine two bonds with identical duration (same first-order rate sensitivity). The high-convexity bond outperforms in all interest rate scenarios — it falls less when rates rise and rises more when rates fall. This better performance has value, so rational investors bid its price up (accepting a lower yield) until the additional yield given up equals the value of the convexity advantage. Practical implication: Portfolio managers actively seek high-convexity bonds during periods of high rate volatility, as the asymmetric payoff is most valuable when large rate moves are likely. Zero-coupon bonds have high convexity for their maturity; callable bonds have negative convexity (the issuer's call option works against the bondholder) — callable bonds must compensate with higher yields.         Dollar loss ≈ $50,000,000 × 0.12 = −$6,000,000 (b) Why actual loss is smaller — convexity: Duration provides a linear (tangent line) approximation to the curved price-rate relationship. Because all bonds are convex, the actual price curve lies ABOVE the linear approximation when rates change. A 1.5% rate rise is a large move — convexity's correction is significant. The actual portfolio loss will be less than $6 million because the price-rate curve bends favorably for the holder. To get the precise estimate, add the convexity correction: ΔP/P ≈ −MD×Δr + ½×Convexity×(Δr)², where the convexity term is positive. (c) Risk reduction strategies:
        • Shorten duration — sell long-maturity, low-coupon bonds; buy short-maturity, high-coupon bonds. Reduces price sensitivity.
        • Duration matching (immunization) — match asset duration to liability duration so rate changes affect both sides equally.
        • Interest rate derivatives — use interest rate swaps (pay fixed, receive floating) or futures to hedge rate exposure without selling bonds.

The current market price is below fair value. An investor buying at this price earns a return (YTM) that exceeds what is required to compensate for the bond's risk. This is a bargain — the bond offers more return than necessary for its risk level. (b) What happens in an efficient market: Sophisticated investors spot the underpricing and buy the bond. This buying pressure drives the market price up, which reduces the YTM (lower price → higher yield, but wait — higher price → lower YTM). As price rises to fair value, YTM falls until YTM = required rate. The mispricing disappears rapidly — in liquid markets, within seconds to minutes. This is the self-correcting mechanism of market efficiency. (c) Why mispricing can persist in the real world:
        • Illiquidity — in thinly traded markets (certain corporate or municipal bonds), there may be no ready buyers even if the bond is underpriced. Trading costs may exceed the profit from exploiting the mispricing.
        • Transaction costs — commissions, bid-ask spreads, and taxes can make exploiting small mispricings unprofitable.
        • Information asymmetry — some investors may know the bond is risky for reasons not publicly available (e.g., inside knowledge of financial distress), so what appears mispriced is actually fair-priced for informed investors.
        • Regulatory constraints — some investors (pension funds, insurance companies) are restricted from buying certain securities regardless of valuation.
        • Short-selling restrictions — it's easier to exploit underpricing (buy the asset) than overpricing (requires short-selling, which has friction and risk).
