title: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy
subtitle: Financial Markets & Institutions
content: How the Fed is structured, what tools it uses to shape the economy, and how monetary policy flows through interest rates and the money supply.

title: 1. The Federal Reserve System: Structure and Purpose
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The Federal Reserve System (FRS), commonly called "the Fed," is the central bank of the United States, established by Congress under the Federal Reserve Act of 1913. Its creation came after a series of devastating financial panics — most notably the Panic of 1907 — that exposed the fragility of the U.S. banking system and demonstrated the need for a lender of last resort that could inject liquidity into the system during crises. Background: Why a Central Bank?
          Before the Fed existed, bank runs were common. When depositors feared a bank was insolvent, they would rush to withdraw funds, causing healthy banks to fail too. A central bank solves this by standing ready to lend to banks that are fundamentally sound but temporarily illiquid — breaking the panic cycle. The Fed is designed as an independent central bank, meaning it operates free from day-to-day political pressure, even though it was created by Congress and remains accountable to it. This independence is crucial: a central bank that bows to political pressure might print money to finance government spending, causing inflation. The Fed's structure enforces a long-term orientation over short-term political considerations. The Fed carries out four major functions: (1) conducting monetary policy, (2) supervising and regulating depository institutions, (3) maintaining the stability of the financial system, and (4) providing payment and other financial services to the U.S. government, the public, financial institutions, and foreign official institutions.

title: The Three-Tier Structure
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The Fed is organized across three tiers. At the foundation are the 12 Federal Reserve Banks (FRBs), each serving a geographic district — located in Boston, New York, Philadelphia, Cleveland, Richmond, Atlanta, Chicago, St. Louis, Minneapolis, Kansas City, Dallas, and San Francisco. Each Reserve Bank acts as a depository institution for the commercial banks in its district, operates under general supervision of the Board of Governors, and has its own 9-member board of directors. Nationally chartered banks are required to become members of the FRS. At the apex sits the Board of Governors in Washington, D.C., composed of seven members appointed by the President and confirmed by the Senate. Each member serves a single nonrenewable 14-year term — one expires every two years. The staggered, non-renewable structure is a deliberate design to insulate members from political pressure: they cannot be reappointed, so they have no incentive to please whoever appointed them. The Board's primary responsibilities are formulating and conducting monetary policy and supervising and regulating banks. Common misconception
          Many students think the Chair of the Fed serves 14 years. In fact, the Chair is a member of the Board who serves a separate 4-year renewable term as Chair. The Board member's term is 14 years; the Chair designation rotates on a 4-year cycle. The third key body is the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), the primary monetary policy-making body. It consists of all 7 Board of Governors members plus 5 of the 12 FRB presidents — the President of the New York Fed serves permanently (given New York's centrality to financial markets), and the other four seats rotate among the remaining 11 Reserve Bank presidents. The FOMC must meet at least four times per year in Washington, D.C., but in practice typically meets eight times annually. Its mandate is to promote full employment, economic growth, price stability, and a sustainable pattern of international trade. The Beige Book
          Before each FOMC meeting, each Reserve Bank gathers qualitative economic intelligence from business contacts in its district. These reports are compiled into the Beige Book, which gives FOMC members a ground-level view of regional economic conditions to complement the quantitative data they review.

title: 2. Functions of Federal Reserve Banks
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Beyond their role in monetary policy, the 12 Federal Reserve Banks carry out a broad range of operational and regulatory functions that keep the financial system running smoothly. On the monetary policy side, each FRB sets and changes the discount rate (subject to Board approval) and manages its discount window through which loans are made to depository institutions. For supervision and regulation, each FRB has supervisory and regulatory authority over state-chartered member banks and bank holding companies in its district — a shared responsibility with other regulators like the FDIC and OCC. The FRBs also handle critical payment infrastructure: they issue new currency, operate the central check-clearing system that routes interbank transactions, and link member banks electronically through the Federal Reserve Communications System for wire transfers. They serve as the commercial bank for the U.S. Treasury — managing the government's checking account, issuing and redeeming Treasury securities, and processing tax payments. Finally, each FRB employs professional economists to conduct research services that inform both regional and national policy decisions.

title: 3. The Federal Reserve's Balance Sheet
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Understanding the Fed's balance sheet is essential for grasping how monetary policy works mechanically. Like any bank, the Fed has both assets and liabilities, but they are unique because the Fed can create and destroy money.

title: Liabilities: The Monetary Base
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The Fed's two major liabilities are currency in circulation (the paper money and coins in people's wallets and cash registers) and bank reserves (the deposits that commercial banks hold at their local Federal Reserve Bank). These two together constitute the monetary base (also called "high-powered money" or M0) — the raw foundation of the entire money supply. Total reserves are divided into two categories: required reserves are the minimum amount the Fed legally mandates banks hold against their deposit liabilities, and excess reserves are any reserves held above and beyond that requirement. The distinction matters enormously for monetary policy, because only excess reserves are "available" for banks to lend out — and it is excess reserves that the Fed targets when implementing policy. Supplementary context
          In March 2020, the Fed reduced reserve requirements to 0% for all depository institutions, effectively eliminating required reserves as a binding constraint. This was partly a COVID-19 emergency measure and partly a recognition that banks already held massive excess reserves following the 2008–09 financial crisis. The concept still matters theoretically, and reserve requirements remain a policy tool, but they are no longer actively used to regulate credit creation.

title: Assets: What the Fed Owns
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The Fed's primary assets are Treasury and government agency securities — U.S. government bonds and the debt of government-sponsored enterprises like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. These are the securities the Fed buys and sells in open market operations. The Fed also holds Treasury currency, gold and foreign exchange, and a small amount of interbank loans (loans to commercial banks through the discount window). While interbank loans represent a small fraction of total assets, they play an outsized role in implementing monetary policy because they directly affect individual banks' reserve positions. Quantitative Easing (QE)
          During the 2008 financial crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic, the Fed dramatically expanded its balance sheet through "large-scale asset purchases" — buying not just Treasury securities but also mortgage-backed securities. This is QE: the Fed creates new bank reserves and uses them to purchase assets, injecting liquidity into the financial system when conventional interest-rate policy hits the zero lower bound. QE expanded the Fed's balance sheet from roughly $900 billion in 2008 to over $8 trillion by 2022.

title: 4. Monetary Policy Tools
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The Fed has three primary instruments for implementing monetary policy, each operating through a different channel. All three ultimately work by influencing the level of bank reserves, which in turn affects the money supply and interest rates throughout the economy. The core mechanism: The Fed influences either the demand for or the supply of excess reserves at depository institutions — and in turn, the money supply and interest rates.

title: Tool 1: Open Market Operations (Primary Tool)
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Open market operations (OMOs) are the Fed's primary and most frequently used monetary policy instrument. They involve the purchase or sale of U.S. government securities (primarily Treasury bills and bonds) in the open market. When the FOMC decides on a monetary target or interest rate target, it issues a policy directive to the Federal Reserve Board Trading Desk at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York (FRBNY). The manager of the Trading Desk then instructs traders on the daily amounts of securities to buy or sell. OMOs work by directly changing the level of bank excess reserves. When the Fed purchases securities from a bank or broker-dealer, it credits the seller's reserve account at the Fed — injecting reserves into the banking system. More reserves mean banks have more funds available to lend, expanding the money supply and pushing interest rates (especially the fed funds rate) down. Conversely, when the Fed sells securities, it debits the buyer's reserve account — draining reserves, tightening credit, and pushing interest rates up. Connect to the Balance Sheet
          When the Fed buys $1 billion in Treasury bonds, its assets increase by $1 billion (more securities), and its liabilities increase by $1 billion (more bank reserves). The monetary base expands. This is how the Fed literally creates money. OMOs are preferred over other tools for several reasons: they are flexible (small or large, easily reversed), frequent (daily adjustments are possible), and precise (the Fed knows exactly how many reserves will be affected). They are the primary determinant of changes in bank excess reserves in the banking system.

title: Tool 2: The Discount Rate
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The discount rate is the interest rate that Federal Reserve Banks charge on loans to financial institutions in their districts, made through the discount window. Raising the discount rate signals a desire for tighter monetary conditions and higher market interest rates; lowering it signals an expansionary stance. Despite its visibility, the Fed rarely uses the discount rate as an active policy tool for two reasons. First, it is difficult to predict how much discount window borrowing will change when the rate changes — banks may borrow more or less than expected. Second, because of its "signaling" importance, a discount rate change tends to have outsized and sometimes disruptive effects on financial markets, making it a blunt instrument. Since January 2003, the Fed offers three distinct discount window lending programs: Common misconception
          The discount rate is NOT the same as the federal funds rate. The fed funds rate is the rate banks charge each other for overnight reserve loans in the private interbank market. The discount rate is what the Fed charges banks that borrow directly from the Fed. They move together directionally, but they are different rates — and the discount rate is typically set above the fed funds rate to discourage routine borrowing from the Fed.

title: Tool 3: Reserve Requirements
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Reserve requirements (also called reserve ratios) specify the minimum percentage of transaction deposits (checking accounts) that depository institutions must hold as reserves — either as vault cash or as deposits at the Fed. Historically, this was a powerful lever: lowering the reserve requirement frees up funds for banks to lend, expanding the money supply through a multiplier process; raising it contracts lending and the money supply. The money multiplier describes how an initial change in reserves propagates through the banking system. When a bank receives a $100 deposit and must hold 10% ($10) as a required reserve, it lends out $90 — which becomes a deposit at another bank, which then holds $9 and lends $81, and so on. The total expansion of deposits is: The Fed very rarely uses reserve requirements as an active monetary policy tool because changes are disruptive (affecting all banks simultaneously) and because the full multiplier effect rarely materializes in practice (banks hold excess reserves, and not all loans become deposits in the same system).

title: 5. The Federal Funds Rate and Interest on Reserves
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Depository institutions with more reserves than they need at the end of the day lend those excess reserves overnight to banks that are short on reserves. This interbank market for reserves is the federal funds market, and the rate at which these overnight loans trade is the federal funds rate (fed funds rate) — one of the most closely watched interest rates in the world. The fed funds rate is a benchmark interest rate: changes in it ripple through virtually all other interest rates in the economy — mortgages, car loans, credit cards, corporate borrowing — because it sets the baseline cost of short-term money. The Financial Services Regulatory Relief Act of 2006 authorized the Fed to pay interest on reserve balances, beginning in 2008. This created two key rates: Interest on Required Reserves (IORR) and Interest on Excess Reserves (IOER). IOER functions as an effective floor for the fed funds rate: no bank will lend reserves at a rate below what the Fed itself pays to hold those reserves. This gave the Fed a new tool to keep the fed funds rate within its target range without having to drain all excess reserves from the system. How the Fed Hits Its Rate Target
          The Fed now uses a "corridor system" with IOER as the floor and the primary credit discount rate as the ceiling. The fed funds rate trades between these two bounds. The Fed adjusts both bounds when it wants to change its target range, making reserve management much more precise than under the old "scarce reserves" regime.

title: 6. Expansionary vs. Contractionary Monetary Policy
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The Fed's three tools work in both directions. Understanding which combinations of actions constitute easing vs. tightening is fundamental to this chapter. • Open market purchases of securities
            • Discount rate decreases
            • Reserve requirement ratio decreases
            Result: Money supply ↑, interest rates ↓, credit availability ↑, economic activity ↑ • Open market sales of securities
            • Discount rate increases
            • Reserve requirement ratio increases
            Result: Money supply ↓, interest rates ↑, credit tightens, inflation pressure ↓ The transmission from policy action to economic outcome is not instantaneous — economists estimate that monetary policy operates with "long and variable lags" of 12–18 months before its full effects are felt. This makes central banking as much an art as a science: the Fed must act today based on where it expects the economy to be a year or more from now.

title: 7. Money Supply vs. Interest Rate Targeting
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A fundamental constraint in monetary policy is that the Fed cannot simultaneously control both the money supply and the interest rate — it must choose one as its operating target, allowing the other to fluctuate freely. This is because money supply and interest rates are linked through money demand: for a given demand curve, fixing the quantity of money determines the price (rate), and fixing the price requires letting the quantity adjust. If the Fed targets the money supply (a quantity target), it sets a specific level or growth rate for a monetary aggregate and lets market forces determine the interest rate. Interest rates will fluctuate with shifts in money demand. If the Fed targets an interest rate (a price target, e.g., the fed funds rate), it stands ready to buy or sell as many securities as needed to keep rates at the target. Bank reserves and the money supply must adjust freely to keep rates on target. In 1993, the Fed formally adopted the federal funds rate as its primary target variable. After each FOMC meeting, the Fed simply announces whether the target range has been raised, lowered, or held — a simple, transparent communication that financial markets can quickly price in.

title: The Taylor Rule
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The guiding principle behind how the Fed sets its interest rate target is the Taylor Rule, named after economist John Taylor. It prescribes what the short-term interest rate should be based on three conditions: In plain English: if inflation is running above target, the Fed should raise rates. If the economy is operating below full employment (a negative output gap), the Fed should cut rates. The rule gives equal weight (0.5) to both the inflation gap and the output gap — reflecting the Fed's dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment.

title: 8. Money Market Equilibrium and Interest Rates
subtitle: Chapter 4: The Federal Reserve & Monetary Policy · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The aggregate demand for real money balances can be expressed as: Three factors shift money demand: a higher interest rate (R) reduces money demand (higher opportunity cost of holding non-interest-bearing cash), a higher price level (P) increases nominal money demand (more cash needed for the same transactions), and a higher real income (Y) increases money demand (more transactions to finance). Money market equilibrium is achieved when Ms/P = L(R, Y). The interest rate adjusts to clear the market: an excess supply of money drives rates down (people dump money for bonds, bidding bond prices up and yields down); an excess demand for money drives rates up (people sell bonds to get cash, pushing bond prices down and yields up). Imagine you and your friends are all playing a board game with Monopoly money. The bank (the Fed) decides to buy one of your houses — your "Treasury bond" — and pays you with freshly printed Monopoly bills. You now have more cash. You can spend it, or lend it to a friend who then spends it. Suddenly, more money is flowing around the table than before. That's it — the Fed "buys" something real (a bond) and pays with new money it creates out of thin air. The Monopoly bank has a fixed pile of bills. The real Fed doesn't — it can create reserves electronically, limitlessly. Also, Monopoly money doesn't cause inflation because it can't buy real goods. In the real world, too much money chasing too few goods does cause inflation. When the Fed buys a Treasury security from a bank, it credits that bank's reserve account at the Fed — increasing the bank's excess reserves. No physical currency changes hands; the Fed simply types a larger number into the bank's reserve ledger. These new reserves are a liability on the Fed's balance sheet and an asset on the bank's. The bank now has more excess reserves available to lend, expanding the money supply through the deposit multiplier process. You deposit $100 at Bank A. Bank A has to keep $10 (10%) but lends out $90 to someone. That someone deposits $90 at Bank B. Bank B keeps $9 and lends out $81. Bank C gets $81, keeps $8.10, lends $72.90... This chain goes on and on. By the end, your original $100 deposit has supported $1,000 worth of deposits in the banking system. The whole system expanded because banks keep recycling the money. That's the multiplier — one dollar of new reserves supports many dollars of deposits. The theoretical multiplier assumes (1) banks lend out every dollar of excess reserves and (2) all loans return to the banking system as deposits. Neither is always true. Banks hold excess reserves as a safety cushion, and some money "leaks" into cash holdings outside banks. The money multiplier is 1 / RRR. With a 10% reserve requirement, a $1 billion open market purchase can theoretically expand the money supply by $10 billion through repeated rounds of lending and deposit creation. A decrease in the reserve requirement increases the multiplier (and thus money supply); an increase decreases it. Imagine a water tank (the money supply) connected to a hose (interest rates). The water pressure in the hose depends on how much water is in the tank. If you fix the amount of water, the pressure is determined by physics — you can't also independently fix the pressure. OR: you can set the pressure (interest rate) and let as much water in as needed to maintain that pressure — but then you lose control of how much water is in the tank. You control ONE thing: either the amount, or the pressure. Not both simultaneously. Money demand isn't fixed like water physics — it shifts with income, prices, and expectations. So the "pressure" (interest rate) can change even if the Fed doesn't change the money supply. This makes the trade-off even harder to manage. Money supply (Ms) and interest rates (R) are linked through money demand: Ms/P = L(R,Y). For a given demand curve, fixing Ms pins R; fixing R requires Ms to adjust. If the Fed targets the money supply, it sets a quantity and lets the market discover the price (interest rate). Since 1993, the Fed has chosen to target the federal funds rate, accepting fluctuations in bank reserves and the money supply. Picture a car cruise control. If you're going too fast (too much inflation), it slows you down (raises interest rates). If you're going too slow (unemployment too high, economy underperforming), it speeds you up (cuts rates). The Taylor Rule is just a formula for the cruise control — it tells the Fed driver how much to press the gas or brake based on two readings: the speedometer (inflation) and whether you're behind or ahead of schedule (jobs/output). Cruise control responds immediately; monetary policy has lags of 12–18 months. Also, cruise control doesn't have a "zero" problem — rates can't go below zero (in normal times), so when the Taylor Rule prescribes a negative rate, the Fed is "stuck" and must use unconventional tools like QE. The Taylor Rule: i = r* + π + 0.5(π − π*) + 0.5(Y − Y*). The fed funds rate target (i) equals the equilibrium real rate (r*) plus actual inflation (π), plus half the inflation gap (actual minus target), plus half the output gap (actual minus potential). It provides a systematic framework for evaluating whether monetary policy is too tight or too loose. 1913 / Federal Reserve Act / Panic. The Panic of 1907 caused massive bank failures and a severe credit crunch, convincing Congress that a central banking institution was needed to prevent future catastrophes. The monetary base = currency in circulation + total bank reserves (required + excess). It represents the fundamental "raw material" of the money supply — before the multiplier effect. FALSE. The federal funds rate is the rate banks charge each other in the private interbank overnight lending market. The rate the Fed charges banks is the discount rate, which is typically set above the fed funds rate. (a) Multiplier = 1/0.08 = 12.5(b) Theoretical max = $200M × 12.5 = $2.5 billion Both gaps point toward raising rates. The inflation gap premium would be 0.5 × (5% - 2%) = +1.5%, and the output gap premium would be 0.5 × (+1%) = +0.5%. Total premium over neutral rate = +2.0 percentage points. 1. Cut the fed funds rate target (OMOs) to reduce borrowing costs.2. Cut the discount rate to encourage bank borrowing.3. Large-scale OMO purchases to inject massive reserves.4. Quantitative Easing (QE) if rates hit zero to push down long-term rates. Monetary Base Currency + bank reserves Excess Reserves Reserves above required minimum Fed Funds Rate Interbank overnight rate IOER Interest on excess reserves (floor) OMOs Primary tool (buy/sell securities) Taylor Rule framework for setting rates The three monetary policy tools and their directions. Fed funds rate vs. discount rate (bank-to-bank vs. Fed-to-bank). Money multiplier formula: 1/RRR. Taylor Rule structure (inflation gap + output gap). Money supply vs. interest rate targeting trade-off.

title: Chapter Summary

content: Core concepts, definitions, and key mechanics from the lecture slides.

title: What Are Money Markets?
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Money markets are financial markets that trade debt securities or instruments with maturities of less than one year. They arise because the immediate cash needs of individuals, corporations, and governments do not always coincide with their cash receipts. Once issued, money market instruments trade in active secondary markets, giving investors the ability to convert holdings to cash quickly. Three Universal Characteristics
          All money market instruments share: (1) generally sold in large denominations ($1M–$10M); (2) low default risk; (3) original maturity of one year or less.

title: Yield Conventions
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Money market yields are quoted in two different ways, and understanding the distinction is critical for comparing instruments. Why Two Conventions?
          The discount yield (360-day) is a market convention inherited from pre-computer banking. The BEY (365-day) is considered more accurate for comparing with bond returns. The BEY is always slightly higher than the discount yield for the same security.

title: Treasury Bills (T-Bills)
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Treasury Bills are short-term U.S. government obligations sold to fund government budget deficits and refinance maturing debt. They are also the primary tool the Federal Reserve uses to implement monetary policy via open market operations. Original maturities: 4, 13, 26, and 52 weeks Issued in multiples of $100 Virtually default-risk free; minimal interest rate and liquidity risk Sold at a discount from face value; return comes from the difference between purchase price and face value at maturity The U.S. Treasury sells new T-Bills through a formal auction. Bids may be competitive — specifying the desired amount and discount yield, typically used by large investors and government securities dealers — or noncompetitive, limited to $5M per bidder, specifying only the desired face value. Noncompetitive bidders accept whatever yield clears the auction. The secondary market for T-Bills is the largest of any U.S. money market security. 24 primary government securities dealers are designated to purchase most competitively bid T-Bills and maintain an active secondary market. Transactions between primary dealers are conducted over the Federal Reserve's wire transfer service — Fedwire. Around 500 smaller dealers also trade in this market.

title: Federal Funds
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Federal funds (fed funds) are short-term funds transferred between financial institutions, usually overnight. Commercial banks trade excess reserves held at their local Federal Reserve Bank. The fed funds rate is determined by supply and demand for these reserves, as well as the Federal Reserve's FOMC operations. Counterparty Risk in Fed Funds
          The primary risk of the interbank lending system is that the borrowing bank does not pledge collateral. Loans are typically oral agreements between correspondent banks for millions of dollars — entirely unsecured.

title: Repurchase Agreements (Repos)
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A repurchase agreement (repo or RP) involves the sale of securities by one party to another with a promise to repurchase them at a specified price on a specified date. Repos are collateralized — the seller holds securities to back the transaction — and a haircut is applied, meaning the loan is slightly smaller than the market value of pledged collateral. Overnight repos: one-day maturity Term repos: longer maturities Reverse repo: the purchase side — the party buying the securities with a promise to sell them back

title: Commercial Paper
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Commercial paper (CP) is an unsecured short-term promissory note issued by a company to raise short-term cash, commonly for working capital. It is one of the largest money market instruments by dollar volume (~$1.1 trillion outstanding as of Dec 2019). Companies with strong credit ratings can borrow more cheaply via CP than bank loans. Denominations: $100,000; $250,000; $500,000; $1M Maturities: 1–270 days (most common: 20–45 days) Quoted on a discount basis (360-day year) Sold directly via issuer's sales force or indirectly through brokers/dealers (more expensive) Asset-Backed Commercial Paper (ABCP) & the 2008 Crisis
          In July 2007, $1.19T of $2.16T total CP outstanding was ABCP — collateralized by financial assets, largely mortgage-backed securities. When subprime mortgages deteriorated, billions in ABCP were tainted, and outstanding CP levels continued to fall even after markets stabilized.

title: Negotiable Certificates of Deposit (CDs)
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A negotiable CD is a bank-issued, fixed-maturity, interest-bearing time deposit that is negotiable — it can be traded in secondary markets any number of times. It is a bearer instrument, meaning whoever holds it at maturity receives principal and interest. Denominations: $100,000–$10M (most common: $1M) Common maturities: 1, 2, 3, 6, and 12 months Interest rates quoted using a 360-day year Large, well-known banks offer slightly lower rates than smaller banks

title: Banker's Acceptances (BAs)
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A banker's acceptance is a time draft payable to a seller of goods, with payment guaranteed by a bank. BAs often arise from international trade transactions. They are payable to the bearer at maturity and trade in secondary markets in round lots of $100,000 and $500,000. Since June 2002, BA levels are included in commercial paper statistics.

title: Feynman Explanations
subtitle: Chapter 5: Money Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Complex concepts explained as if teaching a friend. Click any card to expand. Imagine you buy a $1,000 T-Bill for $980. After 90 days, you get back $1,000 — earning $20. But how do you express that as a yearly rate? That's where the two conventions differ. The discount yield says: "Your $20 profit, divided by the $1,000 face value (not what you paid!), scaled to a 360-day year." This gives a smaller number than reality because you divide by the larger face value and use only 360 days. The BEY says: "Your $20 profit, divided by the $980 you actually paid, scaled to a 365-day year." Since you divide by a smaller number (your actual investment) and use 365 days, BEY is always higher than the discount yield for the same instrument. When comparing a T-Bill to a corporate bond, you must convert the T-Bill's discount yield to BEY — otherwise you're comparing apples to oranges. The BEY is the apples-to-apples number. The U.S. Treasury needs cash and decides to sell $10 billion of 13-week T-Bills. It announces the auction, and investors line up to buy. Competitive bidders (big banks, hedge funds) say: "I'll buy $500 million — but only if the yield is at least 4.95%." They're naming their price. If the auction clears at 4.90%, their bid might not be filled. Noncompetitive bidders (small investors) say: "I want $50,000 worth — I'll take whatever yield the auction produces." They're guaranteed to get their allocation, limited to $5 million per person. The Treasury sorts all competitive bids from lowest yield (highest price) to highest yield (lowest price). It accepts bids starting from the lowest yield until it has raised the $10 billion it needs. The highest yield accepted is the "stop-out rate" — everyone gets that yield, whether they bid higher or lower. Primary dealers buy most of the bills, then sell them to other investors in the secondary market — creating the most liquid money market in the world. A repo is essentially a short-term loan dressed up as a sale-and-repurchase. Here's the mechanic: Bank A needs cash overnight. It sells $100M of T-Bills to Investor B for $99.9M, and simultaneously signs a contract saying "I'll buy them back tomorrow for $100M." Investor B gets securities (collateral) plus a small return ($0.1M) overnight. Bank A gets the $99.9M cash it needs. The "haircut" — the difference between market value of collateral ($100M) and the loan amount ($99.9M) — protects Investor B if Bank A defaults. The same transaction looks like a "reverse repo" from Investor B's perspective: they're buying securities with a promise to sell them back. One party's repo is always the other party's reverse repo. Repos are how large financial institutions manage daily cash balances. The repo market is enormous — trillions of dollars daily — and its stability is crucial to the functioning of bond markets. Every commercial bank must hold a certain level of reserves at the Fed. At the end of each day, some banks find they have more reserves than required; others find they have too few. A bank with excess reserves will call a "correspondent bank" — a bank it has a relationship with — and offer to lend those reserves overnight. The borrowing bank pays the fed funds rate for the use of the money, then returns the reserves the next morning. These loans are typically completely unsecured — no collateral, just a phone call or electronic instruction. Amounts are often in the millions. The trust between correspondent banks is the only guarantee. This is why the fed funds market is considered a measure of financial system confidence. The Federal Reserve influences the fed funds rate by adding or draining reserves from the system through open market operations. When the Fed buys T-Bills, it adds reserves (pushing the rate down). When it sells T-Bills, it drains reserves (pushing the rate up). Regular commercial paper is just an IOU from a company. Asset-backed commercial paper adds a twist: a special vehicle issues the paper, and the collateral backing it is a pool of other financial assets — theoretically reducing risk by diversification. By mid-2007, ABCP had grown to $1.19 trillion of the $2.16 trillion CP market. The collateral backing this paper was heavily concentrated in mortgage-backed securities, many tied to subprime home loans. When U.S. housing prices started falling in 2006–2007, those subprime mortgages began defaulting. Suddenly nobody knew what ABCP was really worth — the assets backing it were toxic. Investors refused to roll over (renew) their ABCP holdings. The market froze almost overnight. Money market instruments are supposed to be boring — short-term, low-risk, liquid. ABCP violated that promise by stuffing long-duration, high-risk, illiquid assets into a short-term wrapper. The mismatch between the instrument's apparent safety and the actual risk of its collateral was catastrophic.
